nativeRange.habitat: Mudskippers inhabit intertidal mudflats and mangrove estuaries along the West African Atlantic coast from Senegal to Angola. They spend the majority of their time out of the water on exposed mud, retreating to waterline burrows when conditions require.

decorPreferences: large sloping land area (at least 50% of tank footprint) of moistened sandy-clay substrate
shallow brackish water section (5–10 cm deep) for bathing and gill-wetting
mangrove roots or driftwood for basking and territorial marking
tight-fitting lid essential — strong escape artists
high humidity maintained in air space above water and land (80–90%)
burrowing substrate (soft mud or sand-clay mix, minimum 10 cm deep) for territorial burrows
gentle filtration — low water flow; sump or hang-on filter kept to minimal surface agitation

diet.notes: Mudskippers are obligate insectivores and invertivores in the wild, feeding primarily on live prey. In the aquarium, the staple diet consists of live insects such as crickets, mealworms, waxworms, earthworms, and fruit flies (Drosophila) of an appropriate size. Some specimens will accept frozen bloodworm or frozen brine shrimp when offered with tongs directly onto the land area, but live or fresh prey is strongly preferred and sinking pellets are rarely taken. Feeder insects should be gut-loaded with calcium-rich food before being offered to improve nutritional value. Mudskippers do not naturally hunt in the water column, so all prey should be presented on the land surface.

goodWith: conspecifics in large paludariums with multiple clearly defined territories (with caution)
fiddler crabs (similar biotope, similar brackish requirements — monitor closely)
mangrove killies in the water section (if sufficient land space reduces aggression)
avoidWith: peaceful community fish — incompatible environment requirements
invertebrates that will be eaten (shrimp, small crabs)
any fish requiring fully freshwater or fully marine conditions
slow-moving bottom fish that cannot retreat from territorial displays

imageCandidates.notes: Periophthalmus barbarus on mudflat, clear dorsal fin display visible. Good representative image.
FishBase species photo of P. barbarus. Check current availability on FishBase species page.

summary: The Atlantic Mudskipper (Periophthalmus barbarus) is a genuinely amphibious goby from West African mangrove estuaries that spends most of its time out of the water, breathing through moist skin rather than relying solely on gills. Keeping one requires a specialist paludarium setup with brackish water and substantial land area — this is not a freshwater fish.
careNotes: The Mudskipper is one of the most distinctive fish available in the aquarium trade — genuinely amphibious, it spends the majority of its life out of water on exposed mudflats and mangrove margins, returning to water primarily to wet its gills, reproduce, and thermoregulate. When on land, it breathes through its skin and buccal cavity, provided these surfaces remain moist; maintaining a humid air space (80–90% relative humidity) above the land area is therefore a fundamental requirement of the setup. Keeping Mudskippers successfully demands a paludarium — a tank that combines an aquatic section with a substantial terrestrial one. Roughly 50% of the total tank footprint should be a sloping, accessible land area composed of soft mud or sandy-clay substrate at least 10 cm deep, allowing the fish to construct burrows. The water section should be shallow (5–10 cm), brackish (SG 1.005–1.010, equivalent to approximately 6.5–13 ppt), and warm (24–30°C). A tight-fitting lid is essential — Mudskippers are powerful jumpers and skilled climbers, easily escaping standard aquarium hoods. Mudskippers sold in the trade are sometimes mislabelled as freshwater fish; they are not freshwater-tolerant long-term and will decline in pure freshwater. Brackish conditions should be maintained from the outset. Territorial aggression tends to be intense: males perform erect-dorsal-fin threat displays, chase, and bite conspecifics. A single specimen or a proven pair with a very large footprint (150 L minimum, 200+ L preferred) is strongly advised. Multiple specimens require clearly partitioned territories, and even then ongoing aggression monitoring is important. Mudskippers do not naturally hunt underwater, so prey should always be presented on the land surface. Live insects (crickets, waxworms, fruit flies, earthworms) are the preferred staple; some individuals will accept frozen bloodworm offered with tongs on land. Gut-loading feeder insects with calcium-rich food before offering them improves nutritional completeness. Water quality in the shallow water section can degrade quickly, so frequent partial water changes and efficient filtration with minimal current are recommended.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely achieved and is generally regarded as a specialist-level undertaking. In the wild, males excavate burrows at the waterline and court females with dramatic fin-erecting and jumping displays. Eggs are deposited in air pockets within the burrow and guarded by the male, who actively aerates the chamber by gulping air. Replicating this in captivity requires a very large paludarium with deep, packable substrate, stable brackish water at the burrow entrance, and ample male territorial space. Newly hatched fry are pelagic (free-swimming in the water column) and require live foods such as rotifers and baby brine shrimp. Successful captive breeding has been reported only by dedicated specialist keepers.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Periophthalmus barbarus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (West Africa, intertidal mangroves), water parameters (temperature 24–30°C, pH 7.5–8.5, salinity 1.004–1.010 SG), adult size (to 15 cm TL), paludarium requirements, diet (live invertebrates/insects), temperament (highly territorial), and breeding overview.

additional.name: FishBase — Periophthalmus barbarus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Oxudercidae, order Gobiiformes), adult size (to 25 cm TL reported — maximum wild size; aquarium specimens typically 10–15 cm), native range (Senegal to Angola, Atlantic West African coast), habitat (intertidal mudflats and mangrove), and diet (invertebrates, insects, small fish). Confirmed euryhaline classification.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Mudskipper Care
additional.notes: Detailed husbandry guide; confirmed paludarium requirements (50% land, 50% shallow brackish water), humidity needs (80–90%), live insect diet, escape-proofing requirements, and aggressive territorial behaviour. Noted mislabelling as freshwater fish in trade.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Brackish Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary brackish-fish trade and husbandry context; confirmed brackish salinity requirements and noted mudskipper specialist-only status. General brackish guide rather than species-specific.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Mudskippers: Amphibious Wonders
additional.notes: Species overview article covering amphibious biology, gill/skin respiration mechanism, West African intertidal biotope, territorial behaviour and male burrow-guarding breeding biology, and specialist aquarium requirements. Confirmed insectivore diet and paludarium setup guidance.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely achieved and is considered specialist-level. Males excavate burrows at the waterline, court females with dramatic fin-erecting and jumping displays, and guard the eggs in air-pocket chambers within the burrow. Successfully breeding mudskippers requires a large paludarium with deep, packable substrate, stable brackish water at the burrow entrance, and ample male territorial space. Newly hatched fry are pelagic (free-swimming in the water column) and require live foods such as rotifers and baby brine shrimp.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Requires paludarium (half-water half-land) setup with brackish water (SG 1.005-1.010), terrestrial space, heated humid air, and live insect/feeder diet. Not freshwater despite being sold so. Specialist amphibious-setup commitment.
